channel.title: Posts on Justin Hayes
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Justin Hayes

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 9A Translation
item.description: Sosias and Xanthias, two actors in a comedy, are onstage portraying slaves that stand guard at the front door of a house. They turn to the audience and introduce the play.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 8C Translation
item.description: Dikaiopolis, Euelpides, Peisetairos, and the rhapsode debate Pericles’ reputation and the nature of what is good for Athens.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 8B Translation
item.description: Euelpides and Peisetairos explain the conditions in Athens and what has befallen them such that they want to flee the city.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 8A Translation
item.description: The story returns to Dikaiopolis and the rhapsode as they leave the chaotic scene at the Piraeus and the Long Walls, and head towards the center of Athens.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 7H Translation
item.description: The younger generation of Scythian men mingle with the Amazonian women, and both groups decide to migrate eastward and form a new society.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 7G Translation
item.description: The Amazons suffer losses in fighting with the Greeks, but the survivors escape their captors and sail to Scythia. They skirmish with the Scythians, but the Scythians, upon learning that the Amazons are women, have other plans for the invaders than driving them out by force.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek Section 7D-F Review Translation
item.description: Socrates explores whether or not the opinions of the many are as valuable as those with experience.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek 7F Translation
item.description: When Euthydemos concludes his argument, Dionysodorus makes the case for the contradiction. Sophists were infamous by Socrates’ day for making both sides of an argument appear equally valid.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek 7E Translation
item.description: Continuing the dialogue, Euthydemos leads Kleinias to contradict his previous statement that the clever are the ones who learn, not the ignorant.

item.title: JACT Reading Greek 7D Translation
item.description: Socrates goes to the Lyceum and finds two sophists, Euthydemos and Dionysodoros, speaking among those assembled. He volunteers one of his young students, Kleinias, to engage the sophists in philosophical dialogue.
